chapters.title: The Little Red Hood
chapters.text:
Once upon a time, in a little village at the edge of a great wood, there lived the sweetest little girl that ever was seen. Her grandmother, who loved her dearly, had sewn her a hood and cloak of the brightest red — and it suited her so well that everyone called her only Little Red Riding Hood.
One morning her mother, who had been baking, called the child to her side. 'Your poor grandmother is ill and all alone beyond the wood,' she said, 'and a visit would do her a world of good.'
Mother: “Come here, my darling. Take this basket to Grandmother — a fresh cake and a little pot of golden butter — and see that she wants for nothing.”
Little Red Riding Hood: “I will, Mother! I shall go straight there and cheer her up. Grandmother does so love my visits.”
Mother: “Then go, and my blessing go with you. But keep to the path through the wood, child — do not wander, and never stop to talk with strangers.”
So Little Red Riding Hood kissed her mother, took up the basket, and set off down the lane toward the great dark wood, humming a little tune as she went.

chapters.title: Into the Wood
chapters.text:
The path wound in beneath the tall old trees, where the light fell green and soft. Little Red Riding Hood had not gone far when, out from the shadows, stepped a great grey wolf.
The Wolf: “Good morning, little one. What a fine red hood you wear. And where might such a sweet child be going, all alone in my wood?”
Little Red Riding Hood: “Good morning, sir! I am going to my grandmother's cottage, on the far side of the wood, to bring her a cake and a pot of butter, for she has been ill.”
The Wolf: “And does your grandmother live very far from here?”
Little Red Riding Hood: “Oh, a little way yet — her cottage stands just past the old mill, the very first house beyond the meadow.”
The Wolf: “What a good little girl. But see how the sun is shining, and how the flowers nod in the glade! Surely your grandmother would love a fresh bouquet. Why not gather her a few?”
Little Red Riding Hood: “What a lovely thought! She would smile so to see them. Just a few, and then straight on my way.”
And so, while Little Red Riding Hood turned aside to pick flowers, the wolf ran off by the shortest way — straight and swift toward the grandmother's cottage.

chapters.title: The Sunlit Glade
chapters.text:
The glade was warm and golden. Each time she picked one flower, a lovelier one seemed to bloom just a little further on, and so, step by step, she strayed deeper from the path — and never once saw how long the shadows had grown.
Little Red Riding Hood: “One more for Grandmother's table... and this tall one for the jug by her window. Oh, she will be so pleased with them all!”
But far ahead, the wolf had already reached the cottage. He knocked — tap, tap — and, softening his rough voice, called that he was her grandchild come with cakes. The trusting old woman bade him lift the latch — and in a single bound the wolf sprang in and swallowed her down whole. Then he pulled on her cap and gown, climbed into her bed, and drew the curtains close, and lay very still, waiting.

chapters.title: Grandmother's Cottage
chapters.text:
At last Little Red Riding Hood came to the cottage. The door stood open, and a strange hush lay over everything. She stepped inside, and though she could not say why, a small cold shiver ran through her.
Little Red Riding Hood: “Grandmother? ...How odd you look today. Why, Grandmother, what big ears you have!”
The Wolf: “All the better to hear you with, my dear.”
Little Red Riding Hood: “And Grandmother, what big eyes you have!”
The Wolf: “All the better to see you with, my dear.”
Little Red Riding Hood: “But Grandmother — what big teeth you have!”
The Wolf: “All the better to EAT you with!”
And with a snarl the wolf leapt from the bed and swallowed poor Little Red Riding Hood too. Then, his belly full at last, he sank back upon the pillows and fell fast asleep, snoring loud enough to rattle the shutters.

chapters.title: The Huntsman
chapters.text:
Now a huntsman was passing the cottage upon his round, and he heard the snoring roll out through the open door. 'How loudly the old woman snores,' he thought. 'I had best make sure that all is well.'
The Huntsman: “Grandmother? ...Why — it is that old wolf I have hunted these many seasons! And what has become of the good woman?”
Guessing at once what had happened, the huntsman took his knife and, snip by careful snip, cut open the sleeping wolf. Out stepped Little Red Riding Hood, blinking at the light — and after her came her grandmother, frightened, but quite unharmed.
Little Red Riding Hood: “Oh! How dark and close it was in there! I shall never, ever stray from the path again.”
Grandmother: “Bless you, good huntsman. You have given me back my dear grandchild — and my own life besides.”
The Huntsman: “You are both safe now. And this old rogue shall trouble the wood no more.”
Quickly they filled the wolf's belly with heavy stones, and when he woke and tried to spring away, the weight was too great, and down he tumbled, and troubled no one ever again. And from that day on, whenever Little Red Riding Hood walked through the wood, she kept to the path, held her tongue before strangers, and hurried straight to her grandmother's door.

chapters.title: The Path Well Kept
chapters.text: You set out with an open heart and a basket of cakes, and you learned the hardest lesson the wood can teach: that not every sweet voice is a kind one, and that a child stays safe by keeping to the path. Grandmother is well again, the huntsman is thanked, and the cake — a little squashed — is eaten at last beside the fire. And never again did the girl in the red hood leave the path for a stranger's flattering word.
